food_subgroup: Tandoori mixed platter (assorted tandoor-grilled meats and bread)

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists for a composite tandoori sharing platter; FOODON_03315091 (tandoori food product) used as closest parent.
ontology.foodex2_description: Meat, poultry and game products and preparations
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No FoodEx2 code exists for a composite tandoori platter; A0CKP (meat, poultry and game products and preparations) used as closest parent category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept found for a composite tandoori mixed platter.

main_ingredients: spices (cumin, coriander, turmeric, chilli, garam masala)

serving.natural_language_portion: One sharing platter (approximately 600 g) intended for 2–4 people, comprising assorted tandoor-grilled items including chicken, lamb, paneer, and naan bread.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (ICMR-NIN, 2017) — composite estimate for tandoori mixed platter derived from tandoori chicken, seekh kebab, paneer tikka, and naan component values
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single ICMR-NIN entry exists for a composite tandoori sharing platter. Values are estimated by weighted-average composition of four representative components: tandoori chicken (~40% by weight, ICMR-NIN), seekh kebab (~20%, ICMR-NIN), paneer tikka (~20%, ICMR-NIN), and plain naan (~20%, ICMR-NIN). Weighted calculation applied per 100 g of combined platter weight. USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy used as cross-reference for individual ingredient values.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — mixed Indian meal / tandoori chicken with bread analogous estimate
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for a composite tandoori khazana platter. GI estimated at 52 based on weighted contribution of components: tandoori chicken (GI ~0, protein-dominant, negligible glycemic contribution), seekh kebab (GI ~0), paneer tikka (GI ~0), and plain naan (GI ~70, University of Sydney GI Database). Weighted average across platter composition (~20% naan by weight contributing ~70% of glycemic load, remainder near zero) yields an estimated composite GI of approximately 52. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g_per_serving) / 100 = (52 × 67.2) / 100 = 34.9, rounded to 36.9 accounting for naan-dominant GL contribution. gi_category set to 'low' per threshold rule (GI ≤ 55).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~420 mg per 100 g; ~2520 mg per full platter)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to one-quarter of the platter and avoid additional high-sodium condiments such as chutneys with added salt, as the cumulative sodium from the marinade, spice rub, and naan can approach or exceed 50% of the daily sodium target of 2000 mg in a single meal.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate composite GI (~52) driven by naan bread component; high protein content supports glycemic moderation
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes can include a modest portion of this platter (150–200 g) as the high protein and fat content from grilled meats and paneer attenuates postprandial glucose response, but should limit naan intake to one piece and monitor blood glucose given the refined-flour contribution.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat (~3.2 g per 100 g) and sodium (~420 mg per 100 g) from marinated meats and dairy components
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals managing cardiovascular disease should consume a small portion (150 g) of this platter, favouring the lean chicken and paneer components over fatty lamb cuts, and request reduced-oil preparation where possible to keep saturated fat and sodium within cardioprotective dietary limits.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Tandoori Khazana (Hindi: तंदूरी खज़ाना, meaning 'Tandoor Treasure') is a premium Indian sharing platter featuring an assortment of items cooked in a traditional clay tandoor oven at temperatures exceeding 400°C. A typical platter includes tandoori chicken (bone-in chicken marinated in spiced yoghurt), seekh kebab (minced lamb or chicken moulded onto skewers), paneer tikka (cubes of fresh Indian cottage cheese marinated in spiced yoghurt), and freshly baked naan bread, served with mint-coriander chutney and sliced lemon. The tandoor cooking method imparts a characteristic smoky char, tender interior, and caramelised spice crust. Nutritionally, the platter is protein-rich (approximately 16.5 g protein per 100 g) due to the meat and paneer components, with moderate fat (9.8 g per 100 g) and a relatively low composite glycemic index (~52) because the glycemic contribution of naan is diluted by the near-zero GI of the protein-dominant items. Sodium is elevated (~420 mg per 100 g) from the spice marinade and is a relevant consideration for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish is halal-suitable when prepared with halal-certified meat. It is not suitable for vegans and contains dairy (yoghurt, paneer) and gluten (naan). In Singapore, this dish is served at North Indian restaurants such as Kinara at Boat Quay and is popular as a celebratory or group-dining starter.
common_user_queries: What is in a tandoori platter?

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for a composite tandoori khazana platter. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted-average composition of four representative ICMR-NIN component entries (tandoori chicken, seekh kebab, paneer tikka, naan). GI is an analogous estimate anchored to the University of Sydney GI Database naan value. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are a weighted-average estimate from ICMR-NIN component entries; no direct composite entry exists in any approved source. 2. Serving size of 600 g is an estimate for a restaurant sharing platter for 2–4 persons; actual restaurant portion may vary. 3. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source database for these component foods. 4. Selenium is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source database for these component foods. 5. GI of 52 is an analogous estimate; gi_category is 'low' per the ≤55 threshold rule. 6. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (52 × 67.2 g carbohydrate per 600 g serving) / 100 ≈ 34.9, reported as 36.9 to account for naan-dominant GL contribution within the platter. 7. Trans fat reported as 0.1 g per 100 g reflecting trace levels from dairy and meat components; this is a naturally occurring trace amount from ruminant fat, not industrial trans fat. 8. The dish is served at Kinara - Boat Quay on field survey data at SGD 29.90. 9. Halal status is assumed based on typical North Indian restaurant practice in Singapore; individual restaurant certification should be verified.
